perturbation.original_text: Recipient has had or will have directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient to whom Confidential Information is disclosed or who have access to Confidential Information sig n a Non -Disclosure Agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement and will promptly notify the Discloser in writing of the names of each such person who has signed such agreements after such agreements are signed.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient has had or will have directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient to whom Confidential Information is disclosed or who have access to Confidential Information sign a Non -Disclosure Agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that the Recipient will promptly notify the Discloser in writing of the names of each person who has signed a similar NDA, we remove a critical element of accountability. This is a legal contradiction because under the Data Protection Act 2018, the Discloser must be able to ensure who has access to the Confidential Information to maintain data security and accountability. Removing this notification requirement makes it harder to prove compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(2) of the UK GDPR mandates accountability, requiring data controllers to demonstrate compliance with the principles related to processing personal data. The original clause, requiring notification of who signed NDAs, helps demonstrate compliance by showing who has access to confidential information. Removing this makes it harder for the Discloser to demonstrate accountability.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’).
perturbation.justification: The omission of the notification requirement in the changed text directly contradicts the accountability principle outlined in Article 5(2) of the UK GDPR. The 'Law Explanation' states that the original clause helps demonstrate compliance by showing who has access to confidential information, while removing it makes it harder for the Discloser to demonstrate accountability. Scraped Law Snippet 2 reinforces this, stating that personal data must be 'processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject', which accountability supports. The changed text weakens the ability to demonstrate this transparency, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that it wil l take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have a ny such information, which measures shall include the highest degree of care that Recipient utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that it will take measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure or use of Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons other than those persons authorized hereunder to have any such information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'all reasonable measures' and 'the highest degree of care that Recipient utilizes to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. This potentially contradicts the UK's Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc.) Regulations 2018, which require appropriate measures to be taken to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_citation: The Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc.) Regulations 2018 Section 4(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4(1) of The Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc.) Regulations 2018 implies a duty to take 'reasonable steps' to keep information secret in order to qualify for trade secret protection. By removing the language specifying 'all reasonable measures' and 'highest degree of care', the modified contract weakens this protection and may undermine the Discloser's ability to claim trade secret status.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Recipient to take 'all reasonable measures' and 'the highest degree of care' to protect confidential information. The changed text removes this specific requirement, stating only that the Recipient will take 'measures' to protect the information. The Law Explanation states that Section 4(1) of The Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc.) Regulations 2018 implies a duty to take 'reasonable steps' to keep information secret. The Perturbation Explanation states this change 'weakens the obligation to protect confidential information'. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, and Scraped Law Snippet 1 is incomplete, the Law Explanation combined with the Perturbation Explanation, the Original Text, and the Changed Text make it clear that the removal of the language specifying a higher standard of care weakens the protection and could undermine the ability to claim trade secret status, contradicting the intent of the regulation to protect trade secrets through reasonable measures.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Recipient or its directors, officers, employees, consultants or agents are requested or required by legal process to disclose any of the Confident ial Information, the Recipient shall give prompt notice so that the Discloser may seek a protective order or other appropriate relief.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Recipient or its directors, officers, employees, consultants or agents are requested or required by legal process to disclose any of the Confidential Information, the Recipient may, at its discretion, notify the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall give prompt notice' to 'may, at its discretion, notify', the obligation to notify the Discloser about legally required disclosures is removed. This contradicts the right to be informed as per UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018 which requires data controllers to be informed promptly about any data breach or legal process that may affect the protection of their data or trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 19 of the UK GDPR dictates that the controller must communicate any rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing carried out in accordance with Article 16, Article 17(1) and Article 18 to each recipient to whom the personal data have been disclosed. Removing the mandatory notification hinders the ability of the disclosing party to exercise their rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Art. 19 GDPR – Notification obligation regarding rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability 1 The controller shall communicate any rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing carried out in accordance with Article 16 , Article 17 (1) and Article 18 to each recipient to whom the personal data have been disclosed, unless this proves impossible or involves disproportionate effort. 2 The controller shall inform the data subject about those recipients if the data subject requests it. Suitable Recitals ( 66 ) Right to be Forgotten ← Art. 18 GDPR Art. 20 GDPR → GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates prompt notification to the Discloser ('shall give prompt notice'), ensuring they can seek protective measures. The changed text replaces this with discretionary notification ('may, at its discretion, notify'), removing the mandatory obligation. This omission contradicts the principle of data protection and control, as the data controller must communicate any restriction of processing, according to Art. 19 GDPR. By making notification discretionary, the modified text hinders the Discloser's ability to exercise their rights to rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing, as mentioned in the Law Explanation, which the original contract ensures.

perturbation.original_text: Any materials or documents of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be promptly returned, accompanied by copies of such doc umentation, after the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.changed_text: Any materials or documents of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be returned after the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'promptly' and 'accompanied by copies of such documentation' weakens the requirement for the return of materials and conflicts with potential trade secret protections under UK law. The UK's Trade Secrets Regulations require that owners of trade secrets take active steps to maintain confidentiality; delaying return and removing the copy requirement creates a loophole for misuse or unauthorized retention of sensitive data.
perturbation.law_citation: The Trade Secrets (Enforcement etc.) Regulations 2018, Section 4
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 4 of the Trade Secrets Regulations emphasizes the need for the trade secret holder to demonstrate reasonable steps have been taken to keep information secret. Delaying the return of materials and eliminating the requirement to include copies weakens the argument that reasonable steps were taken to maintain the trade secret, making it more vulnerable to disclosure or misappropriation.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandated the 'prompt' return of materials, accompanied by copies. The changed text eliminates 'promptly' and the requirement for copies. The Perturbation Explanation states this weakens trade secret protections under UK law, as active steps to maintain confidentiality are required. The Law Explanation supports this, noting that delaying the return and omitting copies weakens the argument that reasonable steps were taken to maintain the trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the 'Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018'. Removing 'promptly' and the copy requirement directly undermines the obligation to take active steps to maintain confidentiality, thus creating a legal contradiction concerning the safeguarding of trade secrets under UK law.
